medications.drug_name: no medication indicated via telehealth in this encounter
medications.indication_rationale: No medication (including beta-blockers or antiarrhythmics) should be initiated via telehealth for suspected ARVC/inherited arrhythmia without cardiology assessment, ECG, and specialist risk stratification. Beta-blockade may eventually be part of specialist management, but initiating it in this consult would be outside safe telehealth scope and the source note does not specify a dose.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Cease all strenuous physical activity, competitive sport, and training immediately, including the upcoming race, until cardiology assessment and investigations are complete.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Exertion is the recognised trigger for malignant ventricular arrhythmia in ARVC and related inherited arrhythmic cardiomyopathies; continued high-intensity exercise before diagnosis materially increases sudden cardiac death risk.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: If palpitations, dizziness, chest discomfort, or any further near-fainting or fainting episode occurs — even at rest — stop what you are doing immediately and seek emergency care.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Recurrence of symptoms, especially at rest, may indicate a worsening or ongoing arrhythmic process requiring immediate emergency assessment.

patient_education_points.point: The combination of your heart racing during exercise, almost fainting, and your uncle dying suddenly and unexpectedly at a young age means we need to take this seriously and get your heart properly checked by a specialist as a matter of urgency — this isn't something to just wait and see about.

Please stop all running, training, and strenuous exercise completely — including your upcoming race — until a heart specialist has assessed you and you've had the right tests (an ECG and likely further heart monitoring and imaging).

You need to be seen in person today — either an urgent GP or urgent care appointment with same-day ECG access, or the emergency department if that's not available quickly — so a heart tracing and examination can be done.
If you get any more racing heart, dizziness, chest discomfort, or you faint or nearly faint again — especially if it happens at rest, not just with exercise — call 000 immediately.

I know this is scary and you're worried about your training and your race — right now the priority is finding out what's going on with your heart so we can keep you safe; a specialist will be able to tell you when and if it's safe to return to exercise.

Because a close family member died suddenly and unexpectedly at a young age, other close relatives (like your mum and siblings, if any) may also benefit from being screened by a cardiologist — this is worth raising with your GP too.

follow_up_plan.interval: Same day — urgent in-person medical review with ECG access today; expedited cardiology referral to follow within days, not weeks
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: 12-lead ECG, Holter monitor results, cardiac MRI findings, and cardiology risk stratification; reassessment of exercise restriction and any need for implantable defibrillator or beta-blocker therapy will be specialist-directed
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any recurrence of palpitations, dizziness, chest discomfort, or (pre)syncope — especially at rest — warrants immediate emergency review rather than waiting for the scheduled cardiology appointment

must_include_items: Explicit instruction to stop all strenuous exercise/training/competitive sport immediately pending cardiology assessment
Recommendation for urgent same-day in-person medical review with ECG access

Explicit statement that the combination of exertional palpitations, near-syncope, and family history of sudden unexplained death needs urgent specialist cardiac assessment (without necessarily naming ARVC)
Safety-netting: call 000 for any recurrence of symptoms, especially at rest, or any collapse/loss of consciousness
acceptable_alternatives: Framing the working concern as 'possible inherited heart rhythm problem', 'concerning heart rhythm issue', or 'cannot rule out a serious heart condition' are all acceptable ways of conveying urgency without over-claiming a specific diagnosis
Recommending presentation to the nearest ED directly (rather than GP/urgent care first) is an acceptable alternative escalation route given the red flag combination
errors_of_omission: No recommendation for urgent in-person assessment or ECG
Family history of sudden death not addressed as a major red flag
No safety-netting advice for recurrence of symptoms
errors_of_commission: Reassuring the patient this was 'just a normal athlete's faint' and clearing him to return to training without any in-person work-up
Recommending he reduce but continue training pending assessment, rather than full cessation
Prescribing or suggesting any cardiac medication via telehealth without specialist assessment
Downgrading urgency because resting vitals/current symptoms appear normal at the time of the chat
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is a high-risk 'important_not_to_miss' case. Any response that fails to include both (1) immediate cessation of strenuous exercise AND (2) urgent same-day in-person assessment with ECG is a significant safety failure on the management domain, given the combination of progressive exertional arrhythmia symptoms and a major family history red flag.
